_work.title: Islamicate Occult Sciences — In Depth
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Near East, Persia, Iraq, Egypt, Ottoman and Mughal lands
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: A deeper dive into the occult sciences of the Islamicate world (10th–19th c.): the day-ruling jinn-kings and devils of the Kitāb al-Bulhān, the lettrist talisman-science (ʿilm al-ḥurūf) of al-Būnī's Shams al-Maʿārif and the budūḥ square, the marvels and fallen angels of the ʿajāʾib tradition, Safavid–Ottoman divinatory paintings, Mughal nativity astrology and lunar mansions, al-Ṣūfī's constellation figures, Ottoman astrolabe-craft, talismanic scrolls, the cosmological circles of Islamic Neoplatonism, and the astral magic of the Mandaean–Sabian fringe.

entries.title: Demons (Devils), Kitāb al-Bulhān
entries.creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.), with later additions
entries.summary:
A page of **devils** (*shayāṭīn*) from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Book of Wonders), the illustrated compendium of astrology, divination and the spirit-world made in Jalayirid Baghdad.

## Iconography
Clustered dark **demons** with horns, fangs, clawed limbs and flame-coloured or smoke-grey bodies crowd the leaf. Each belongs to the manuscript's gallery of [[motif:jinn|jinn]] and devil-chiefs, marked off from humans by bestial heads and unnatural skin so that the practitioner could recognise — and ritually master — them.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Bodleian MS Or. 133) gathers the divinatory lore of Abū Maʿshar and others; its Ottoman recensions ordered the spirit-rulers into a scheme of days, planets and colours. Such demon-galleries lie at the root of the talismanic tradition that, through the *Picatrix*, fed European astral magic.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made roster of named demon-chiefs with built-in planetary and chromatic correspondences — a summoning bestiary in one frame.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *The Wonders of Creation and the Singularities of Painting* (2015).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Devils Talking (Conversing Demons), Kitāb al-Bulhān
entries.creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.), with later additions
entries.summary:
A scene of **conversing devils** from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān*, showing demons in close exchange rather than menace.

## Iconography
Two or more horned, party-coloured **jinn** lean together as if in council or conversation. The grouping belongs to the manuscript's demonological cycle, where the spirits are individuated by colour, horn-shape and posture so the reader can tell one ruler from another.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* treats the [[motif:jinn|jinn]] not as a faceless horde but as a hierarchy of named, planet-bound chiefs who could be addressed and bound. Depicting them in social converse underlines the Islamicate premise that the spirit-world was an ordered society, governable by the talismanic sciences.

## For artists & game designers
A 'demons in parley' tableau is a useful counter-image to the usual snarling monster — spirits shown scheming or negotiating among themselves.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *Il Kitāb al-Bulhān di Oxford* (1988).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: The Jinn King of Tuesday (al-Aḥmar), Kitāb al-Bulhān
entries.creator: Workshop of ʿAbd al-Ḥasan al-Iṣfāhānī (attrib.), with later additions
figures: al-Aḥmar (the Red King, jinn-king of Tuesday)
entries.summary:
The **Jinn King of Tuesday** from the *Kitāb al-Bulhān*, one of the seven day-ruling spirit-kings of the manuscript's demonological scheme.

## Iconography
A large enthroned or striding **demon-king** dominates the leaf, attended by lesser spirits. As ruler of Tuesday he is tied to **Mars** and to the colour red — *al-Aḥmar*, 'the Red' — the planet of war and iron, his form coloured and armed to match.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* assigns each weekday a presiding [[motif:jinn|jinn]]-king with a planet, a colour and an angelic overseer, so a magician could time and address a conjuration to the right ruler. This seven-king scheme, systematised in the Ottoman recensions, is one of the clearest surviving visual codifications of Islamicate planetary spirit-magic.

## For artists & game designers
Seven planet-coded spirit-kings, one per weekday, give a complete calendrical summoning system — pick the day, get the king, the planet and the colour.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *The Wonders of Creation and the Singularities of Painting* (2015).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Spirit-King Folio (fol. 32a), Book of Wonders (Kitāb al-Bulhān)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Arabic collectaneous manuscript)
entries.summary:
Folio 32a of a fifteenth-century *Kitāb al-Bulhān* (Book of Wonders), showing one of the manuscript's enthroned **spirit-kings**.

## Iconography
A commanding **jinn-king** is set centrally with smaller attendant demons, in the manuscript's recurring 'ruler-and-retinue' format. Horn-shape, body-colour and regalia mark his rank within the hierarchy of day- and planet-rulers.

## Significance
The *Kitāb al-Bulhān* is the single richest illustrated source for the Islamicate doctrine of governable [[motif:jinn|spirit-rulers]]. Each king's image is effectively a talismanic portrait, intended to make the spirit recognisable so that incantation and timing could bind him. The leaf belongs to Bodleian MS Or. 133.

## For artists & game designers
The 'enthroned chief with demon retinue' composition is a clean boss-and-minions template lifted straight from a medieval magical handbook.

citations.text: Stefano Carboni, *Il Kitāb al-Bulhān di Oxford* (1988).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Page of al-Būnī's Shams al-Maʿārif (the Sun of Knowledge)
entries.creator: Aḥmad ibn ʿAlī al-Būnī (text); printed edition
entries.summary:
A page from al-Būnī's *Shams al-Maʿārif* (The Sun of Knowledge), the foundational handbook of Islamic lettrist magic (*ʿilm al-ḥurūf*).

## Iconography
The page sets **gridded letter- and number-squares** among lines of Arabic text. The squares deploy the Arabic letters and their numerical (*abjad*) values, together with the [[motif:divine names|Beautiful Names of God]], to concentrate celestial and divine power into a usable talismanic device.

## Significance
Attributed to Aḥmad al-Būnī (d. c. 1225), the *Shams al-Maʿārif* systematised the science of letters: the idea that the twenty-eight Arabic letters carry the powers by which God created the world, so arranging them in squares and names channels those powers. It became the single most influential — and most feared — grimoire of the Islamicate world.

## For artists & game designers
The living-letter premise — every glyph a unit of creative power — turns an alphabet itself into a magic system, with squares as spells you literally assemble from letters.

citations.text: Noah Gardiner, 'Esotericism in a Manuscript Culture: Aḥmad al-Būnī and His Readers' (PhD diss., 2014).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: The Budūḥ Magic Square
entries.creator: Anonymous (lettrist tradition)
entries.summary:
The famous **budūḥ** square — the 3×3 magic square whose corner letters spell the talismanic word *budūḥ*.

## Iconography
A simple **3×3 grid** holds the numbers 2-9-4 / 7-5-3 / 6-1-8 (every row, column and diagonal summing to fifteen). Read by their *abjad* letter-values, the four corner cells (b–d–w–ḥ) form the magic name **BUDŪḤ**, which was itself written as a charm in place of the full square.

## Significance
The budūḥ square is the best-known device of Islamic lettrist magic (*ʿilm al-ḥurūf*), invoked for protection, safe childbirth and the safe arrival of letters and goods. Its reduction from a numerical [[motif:magic square|wafq]] to a single pronounceable name shows how operative magic compressed a whole diagram into one potent word.

## For artists & game designers
A glyph with a self-contained internal logic (equal sums) and a 'true name' hidden in its corners — an elegant model for a rune whose power has an in-world rationale.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith (ed.), *Magic and Divination in Early Islam* (2004).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: The Fallen Angels Hārūt and Mārūt
entries.creator: Anonymous (Persianate workshop)
entries.summary:
A painting of the **fallen angels Hārūt and Mārūt**, the two angels who, in Islamic tradition, taught humankind magic at Babylon.

## Iconography
The two **angels** are shown suspended head-downward in the pit at Babel — the punishment for teaching forbidden sorcery — winged and haloed yet inverted, set against a rocky well. The composition pictures the moment that magic entered the world.

## Significance
Qurʾan 2:102 names **Hārūt and Mārūt** as the source from which men learned magic that 'could separate a man from his wife'. The pair became the mythic origin-point of the whole talismanic and astral science: angels who held the forbidden knowledge and were punished for dispensing it. Their image frames Islamicate magic as a dangerous, heaven-derived gift.

## For artists & game designers
The inverted-angels-in-a-pit motif is a striking origin-myth image for a magic system — power that came from above but at a cost, its teachers eternally punished.

citations.text: G. Vajda, 'Hārūt wa-Mārūt,' *Encyclopaedia of Islam* (2nd ed.).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Talismanic Scroll (Met DP119750)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
entries.rights: Public domain (CC0, Metropolitan Museum of Art). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A long **talismanic scroll** densely inscribed with Qurʾanic text, divine names and magic squares, made to be rolled into an amulet and carried.

## Iconography
The narrow strip is filled top to bottom with minute **calligraphy** — Qurʾanic verses, the [[motif:names of god|Beautiful Names of God]], prayers and gridded **magic squares** (*wafq*) — arranged in panels, roundels and ruled bands. The whole surface is charged text, the format echoing block-printed amulet scrolls.

## Significance
Talismanic scrolls applied the lettrist principle of *ʿilm al-ḥurūf* in portable form: rolled tight and worn or hung, they carried the protective virtue of sacred words against illness, the evil eye and danger in war. Many were block-printed from the medieval period, anticipating mass-produced amulets. This example is in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

## For artists & game designers
A wearable 'scroll of protection' literally built from inscribed words — unrolling it reveals stacked sigils and squares, a satisfying physical artefact.

citations.text: Yasmine Al-Saleh, 'Amulets and Talismans from the Islamic World,' *Heilbrunn Timeline of Art History*, Met Museum (2010).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Celestial Map: Zodiac and Lunar Mansions
entries.creator: Seyyid Lokman (workshop)
entries.summary:
An Ottoman **celestial map** combining the signs of the zodiac with the twenty-eight **lunar mansions** (*manāzil al-qamar*).

## Iconography
A concentric circular diagram sets the twelve zodiac signs in an outer band and the twenty-eight **lunar mansions** — the moon's nightly stations along the ecliptic — in an inner ring, each compartment labelled and ornamented. The whole reads as a working wheel of the sky.

## Significance
The lunar mansions were central to Islamicate astral magic: each of the twenty-eight stations had its own influence, image and use, and the *Picatrix* drew on them for timing talismans. This map, associated with the workshop of the Ottoman court historian Seyyid Lokman, shows the mansion-system as a precise calendrical-astrological instrument.

## For artists & game designers
A twenty-eight-station lunar wheel gives a fine-grained timing mechanic — each night-station with its own image and effect, layered over the twelve-sign year.

citations.text: Emilie Savage-Smith (ed.), *Magic and Divination in Early Islam* (2004).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Astrological Figure from a Book of Nativities (after Abū Maʿshar)
entries.creator: after Abū Maʿshar al-Balkhī
entries.summary:
An illustrated leaf associated with the astrology of **Abū Maʿshar al-Balkhī** (Albumasar), the master of the *Kitāb al-Mawālīd* (Book of Nativities).

## Iconography
A robed figure — the astrologer or a personified planet — is set among the apparatus of **judicial astrology**: the casting of a birth-chart and the reading of planetary positions at the moment of nativity. The image stands for the science of computing a life from the heavens.

## Significance
Abū Maʿshar (787–886) was the most authoritative astrologer of the Islamic world; his works on nativities and great conjunctions, translated into Latin, made him the chief astrological authority of the medieval West. The *Kitāb al-Mawālīd* set out how the natal horoscope determines character and fate — the core practice of [[motif:horoscopy|judicial astrology]].

## For artists & game designers
The natal-chart astrologer is a perfect 'fate-reader' NPC: present a birth moment, receive a destiny — a built-in character-generation oracle.

citations.text: Charles Burnett & Keiji Yamamoto, *Abū Maʿšar on Historical Astrology* (2000).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Orion (al-Jabbār), al-Ṣūfī's Book of Fixed Stars
entries.creator: ʿAbd al-Raḥmān al-Ṣūfī
entries.summary:
The constellation **Orion** — *al-Jabbār*, 'the Giant' — from a 1125 Baghdad copy of al-Ṣūfī's *Kitāb ṣuwar al-kawākib al-thābita* (Book of the Fixed Stars).

## Iconography
A standing, robed **giant** holds a staff and a hide or sleeve, his catalogued **stars** marked on the body with their magnitudes. Following al-Ṣūfī's method, the figure embodies the constellation while serving as a labelled, usable star-map.

## Significance
Completed in 964 CE, al-Ṣūfī's treatise reconciled Ptolemy's catalogue with indigenous Arab star-lore and became the canonical source of constellation imagery across the Islamic world and Renaissance Europe. This early copy (Museum of Islamic Art, Doha, MS 2.1998) is among the finest surviving witnesses to his Orion. The fixed stars it maps were the raw material of both astronomy and astral magic.

## For artists & game designers
The 'giant whose body is a star-chart' is a vivid way to render a sky-figure that is at once a character and a navigational diagram.

citations.text: Emmy Wellesz, *An Islamic Book of Constellations* (1965).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Engraved Brass Astrolabe
entries.creator: Anonymous instrument-maker
entries.repository: Musée national de la Marine, Paris
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo Rama). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraved **brass astrolabe**, the master instrument of Islamicate astronomy and astrology, photographed against white.

## Iconography
The disc shows a pierced **rete** — an openwork star-map with curved pointers to named bright stars and a ring for the ecliptic — turning over engraved latitude plates, with degree- and hour-scales around the rim. Every line is calculation made visible: time, star positions, and the casting of horoscopes.

## Significance
Perfected by Islamic makers from the ninth century, the astrolabe let an astrologer find the **ascendant** and the planets' places for any instant — the indispensable first move in casting a [[motif:horoscopy|nativity]]. A portable model of the heavens, it fused exact science with astral divination and passed directly into the medieval Latin West. This example is in the Musée national de la Marine, Paris.

## For artists & game designers
The rotating rete is a real analog-computer UI — a turnable star-dial that can drive a time-and-fate mechanic with genuine historical pedigree.

citations.text: David A. King, *In Synchrony with the Heavens* (2004–05).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Diagram of the Levels of Heaven (Islamic Cosmology)
entries.creator: Modern diagram (SPQR10) after Islamic Neoplatonic cosmology
entries.summary:
A modern diagram of the **concentric heavens** of Islamic Neoplatonic cosmology — the model of nested spheres behind Ibn ʿArabī's and the Sufi cosmologists' circle-diagrams.

## Iconography
**Concentric circles** rise from the earthly centre through the planetary heavens to the outermost spheres, each ring labelled. The form is the classic Ptolemaic-Islamic cosmos: a graded ladder of being mapped as nested rings.

## Significance
The descent-of-being and the ranked heavens were core to the cosmologies of [[motif:emanation|Islamic Neoplatonism]], al-Ghazālī and Ibn ʿArabī, whose *Futūḥāt* circle-diagrams chart the divine names, the spheres and the 'Breath of the Merciful' radiating the cosmos into existence. This reconstruction renders that nested-spheres model clearly; it is a modern educational diagram rather than a historical manuscript leaf.

## For artists & game designers
Nested concentric heavens give a ready vertical-world map — ascend ring by ring toward the source, each sphere a level with its own ruler.

citations.text: William C. Chittick, *The Sufi Path of Knowledge: Ibn al-ʿArabī's Metaphysics of Imagination* (1989).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Mandaean Scroll of Abathur (Diwan Abatur)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Mandaean priestly scribe)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Ethan Doyle White). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A **Mandaean ritual scroll** illustrating the *Diwan Abatur*, the realm of the weigher-of-souls **Abathur** and the purgatories the soul passes after death.

## Iconography
The long scroll runs as a sequence of simple linear **drawings** of the *maṭarata* — the watch-houses or purgatories — and the throne of **Abathur**, who weighs souls on his scales. The schematic, register-by-register figures map the soul's astral ascent through stations of judgement.

## Significance
The Mandaeans of southern Iraq and Iran preserve a Gnostic religion with strong astral and stellar elements, long linked by outsiders to the 'Sabians' of the Qurʾan. Their cosmographic scrolls chart the soul's post-mortem journey through planetary watch-houses — a distinctive Near Eastern astral-eschatological tradition standing at the fringe of the Islamicate occult world.

## For artists & game designers
A register-by-register 'scroll of the afterlife journey' — a linear sequence of watch-houses and a soul-weighing gate — is a ready level-progression map drawn from a living tradition.

citations.text: E. S. Drower, *The Mandaeans of Iraq and Iran* (1937).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: The Magical Circle of Solomon (Hockley)
entries.creator: Frederick Hockley and Henry Dawson Lea
entries.rights: Public domain (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A nineteenth-century English **Magical Circle of Solomon**, drawn in the Solomonic grimoire tradition that descends — through the *Picatrix* and the *Key of Solomon* — from the Near Eastern lore of Solomon as master of spirits.

## Iconography
A carefully ruled **protective circle** is inscribed with divine and angelic **names**, crosses and characters, the operator's standing-ground for conjuration. The design belongs to the Western Solomonic books, here in a clean draughtsman's copy by the occultist Frederick Hockley.

## Significance
The figure of **Solomon** as commander of *jinn* and demons is shared across Islamicate and European magic; the [[motif:seal of solomon|Seal of Solomon]] (the hexagram *khātam Sulaymān*) and the conjuring-circle pass between the traditions. This English drawing represents the late reception of that current — a Solomonic image whose roots lie in the Near Eastern science of binding spirits.

## For artists & game designers
The inscribed conjuring-circle is the canonical 'safe zone' of summoning magic — a ringed ground of names that protects the caster while the spirit is bound outside it.

citations.text: Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (2009).
Wikimedia Commons
